pages.notes: A large glued-in photo (with a pencil-drawn rectangular border/placeholder) covers most of the left page, leaving only the left edge of lines visible below the top paragraph. The top paragraph continues from the previous spread and describes a mine exhibit (candle holders, miners' leather aprons, a German lift installed in 1881). The partial words along the left margin are largely unreadable; 'a Czec[h]' may refer to a Czech person or thing. No handwritten page number visible in top-left corner.

pages.full_text:
Charles picture of Transport Aug 12th Tuesday

    We drove quite a way over very empty countryside. The trees are far enough apart for there to be moss on the ground. We stopped for lunch near an ants' nest, parking in front of someone's garage, which caused her to come up, walking her dog, to enquire when we'd be moving off.
    The countryside must be used by cross-country skiers.
    We came to __Rjukan-Vemork__ which is where the Norwegians had one of their first hydro electric power stations at the turn of the century. The workers' union exhibition showed how important communism was in the area.
    In 1940 the Germans took over the factory to increase the supply of "heavy water" for making the atomic bomb. The factory appeared impregnable, perched on a cliff above a gorge. (The poorer workers
headings: Charles picture of Transport Aug 12th Tuesday
pages.notes: Page number 75 visible in top-right corner. The heading 'Charles picture of Transport' likely refers to a photo on the facing (left) page. Aug 12th Tuesday: within 1989-2003 range, Aug 12th fell on a Tuesday in 1997 and 2003 — cannot confirm year from content alone. The text describes visiting Rjukan-Vemork, Norway (first Norwegian hydro-electric power station and WWII heavy water production site). Text is cut off at bottom ('The poorer workers' continues on next page).
